goal_05.config.title: Shaping the future: Women’s work, unpacked
goal_05.config.abstract: Women continue to face persistent barriers to participate in the labor force. Those who do work are more likely to be in vulnerable employment, earn lower wages, and shoulder domestic and care responsibilities—highlighting the slow and uneven progress toward gender equality at work.
goal_05.config.inputs: Ana Maria Munoz Boudet, Kathleen Beegle
goal_05.content.#0.text: Women’s participation in the labor force
goal_05.content.#1.text: Historically, women have faced persistent barriers to full participation in labor markets and jobs. Social norms prioritized women’s role towards unpaid care and household responsibilities, limiting their access to income-generating work. Many women were also constrained by restricted access to finance to start a business, or to education and skills that kept them out of available jobs.[reference: social-norms] These barriers have shaped women’s participation in the labor force for decades, with lasting consequences for economic growth, household well-being, and gender equality.
goal_05.content.#2.text: In simple terms, the labor force includes everyone who is either working or not working but actively seeking work.[reference: active-work] Globally, around 8 in 10 men between the ages of 15 and 64 are part of the labor force. [reference: wdi-lfp] But what about women? Take a guess in the chart below and see how close you come to the actual share.
goal_05.content.#3.title: How has female labor force participation changed globally relative to male labor force participation?
goal_05.content.#3.subtitle: The global percentage of men participating in the labor force has declined slightly since the 1990s. What’s the story for women?
goal_05.content.#3.source: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), date accessed: January 07, 2025. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SL.TLF.ACTI.FE.ZS, SL.TLF.ACTI.MA.ZS). https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SL.TLF.ACTI.FE.ZS
goal_05.content.#3.instructions: Click or drag to draw a line with your best guess, starting from the female labor force participation rate in 1990 on the left
goal_05.content.#3.encouragement: Keep going! Draw your guess all the way to 2025 on the right
goal_05.content.#3.done: Click the button to see your result. You can still adjust your guess
goal_05.content.#3.good_result: Nice work! Your guess was on average {result} percentage points off the actual female labor force participation rate, which is around 55% today
goal_05.content.#3.ok_result: Not bad! Your guess was on average {result} percentage points off the actual female labor force participation rate, which is around 55% today
goal_05.content.#3.hm_result: Hmm, that could've been better. Your guess was on average {result} percentage points off the actual female labor force participation rate , which is around 55% today
goal_05.content.#3.poor_result: That was poor. Your guess was on average {result} percentage points off the actual female labor force participation rate, which is around 55% today
goal_05.content.#4.text: Since 1990, women’s participation in the labor force has remained largely unchanged, with [emphasis: only half of women between the ages of 15 and 64 engaged in income generating work.]
goal_05.content.#5.text: This represents a substantial gap compared to men—roughly 30 percentage points.[reference: wdi-lfp]
goal_05.content.#7.text: Progress in increasing women’s participation in the labor force has been extremely slow. Progress varies widely across countries and tends to move at different speeds depending on their stage of development. To put this into perspective, consider the trajectory of a “typical” country. Using historical country-level data from 1990 to 2024, we calculated a “typical” path of progress (with more details on the methodology provided on this page).[reference: progress-paper]
goal_05.content.#8.title: Women’s labor force participation is nearly 400 years away from equality
goal_05.content.#8.source: Calculations by World Bank staff based on the progress paper (to be added when working paper available)
goal_05.content.#8.keyFactLabel: Before quality in labor force participation
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#0.text: Now, imagine a country with nearly no women in the labor force.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#1.text: If this country took the typical path of progress, then it would take 850 years to reach 80 percent of women’s labor force participation - the level that reflects today’s global average for men.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#2.text: This means, at just 55 percent in 2024, the global average of women’s labor force participation remains 391 years away from reaching parity with men at 80 percent. So, are there any examples of countries with rapid increases in women’s participation in the labor force?
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#3.text: Türkiye is one of them. In 2015, women’s labor force participation stood at 34 percent.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#4.text: By 2024, it had risen to 41.5 percent—an increase of 6.5 percentage points.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#5.text: In fact, for a typical country, increasing female labor force participation from 34.7 percent to 41.5 percent would take more than 35 years.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#6.text: In other words, Türkiye’s progress was 4 times faster than a typical one, marking notable gains in women’s participation.
goal_05.content.#8.scenes.#7.text: Use the country selector to explore where each country stands today, how fast it has progressed since 2015, and how far it is from parity.
goal_05.content.#9.text: Despite sluggish global progress, countries like Türkiye have achieved substantial gains through targeted policy shifts that enable women’s employment. So, what contributed towards the increase in women’s participation in Turkiye’s labor force?
goal_05.content.#10.text: Drivers for increasing women’s labor force participation
goal_05.content.#11.text: Several factors came together to shape women’s work in Türkiye including a shift towards the industry and services sector, fall in fertility rate, increase in education levels, and a shift in social and cultural norms.
goal_05.content.#13.text: Beginning in the 1990s, the economy underwent a structural transformation, shifting away from agriculture and toward industry and services. Many women had been working in agriculture, especially in their family farms, but as farms shrank, families migrated to cities.[reference: turkey-driver]
goal_05.content.#14.text: Opportunities available in urban labor markets often demanded new skills and formal training, and childcare support was limited. As is common in the early stages of industrialization, men were more likely to acquire the new skills needed, while women—faced with lower levels of education and lack of childcare—were excluded from these opportunities. With little opportunities available to them, many women withdrew from the workforce.[reference: turkey-driver] Between 1990 and 2004, women’s labor force participation fell from 36 percent to 25 percent.[reference: wdi-lfp] ​​
goal_05.content.#15.text: As education levels rose, younger generations of women began to join the labor market in greater numbers, finding opportunities in industry and services.[reference: turkey-scd]
goal_05.content.#16.title: Türkiye’s shift from farms to services reshaped women’s work
goal_05.content.#16.subtitle: Women’s and men’s share of workforce by economic sector (% of population in the workforce by gender)
goal_05.content.#16.textParagraph: Between 1990 and 2020, women in Türkiye increasingly moved out of agriculture as growing industry and service sectors opened up new work opportunities.
goal_05.content.#16.source: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), publisher: ILOSTAT, date accessed: January 07, 2025. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SL.AGR.EMPL.FE.ZS; SL.AGR.EMPL.MA.ZS; SL.IND.EMPL.FE.ZS; SL.AGR.EMPL.MA.ZS; SL.SRV.EMPL.FE.ZS; SL.SRV.EMPL.MA.ZS)
goal_05.content.#18.text: At the same time, social norms and family dynamics were evolving.[reference: turkey-transitions] Fertility rate, or the number of children born per woman, in Türkiye declined faster than the global average— falling to 1.5 by 2023 (compared with 2.2 globally).[reference: wdi-fertility] With fewer children, the childcare barrier was less critical and women had greater ability to pursue jobs.
goal_05.content.#19.title: Türkiye’s fertility rate fell faster than the global average
goal_05.content.#19.subtitle: Fertility rate, total (births per woman)
goal_05.content.#19.textParagraph: In 1960, Türkiye had higher fertility than the global average. Since 1960, fertility rates steadily declined, reaching the global average in the 1990s and dropping below it by 2024.
goal_05.content.#19.source: World Population Prospects, United Nations ( UN ), publisher: UN Population Division; Statistical databases and publications from national statistical offices, National statistical offices; Demographic Statistics, Eurostat (ESTAT). Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.DYN.TFRT.IN) https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.DYN.TFRT.IN
goal_05.content.#21.text: Education played an equally important role: while women’s participation in the labor force has historically been lower than men’s, the gap is widest among those with little or no education, and narrowest among those with advanced degrees.[reference: ilo-education] As more women gained access to higher levels of schooling, their opportunities in the labor market expanded.
goal_05.content.#22.title: Gender gap in Türkiye’s labor force participation is narrowest in the highest level of education
goal_05.content.#22.subtitle: Labor force participation by gender and level of education (% of population by gender and level of education)
goal_05.content.#22.textParagraph: At every level of education in Turkiye, women are less likely than men to work. The gap narrows, however, among people with advanced degrees.
goal_05.content.#22.source: Education and Mismatch Indicators database (EMI), International Labour Organization (ILO), uri: https://ilostat.ilo.org/data/bulk/, publisher: ILOSTAT
goal_05.content.#23.text: From the 2000s onward, these shifts—falling fertility, rising education levels, and gradual changes in social norms—combined with economic growth helped create more jobs for women.[reference: turkey-transitions] As these changes took hold, female labor force participation began to recover, reaching 41 percent in 2024.[reference: wdi-lfp]
goal_05.content.#24.text: This progress shows that narrowing the gap in labor force participation between women and men is possible within a generation when the right social and economic conditions align.[reference: social-norms]
goal_05.content.#26.text: Simply entering the labor force doesn’t guarantee equal opportunity.[reference: gender-distortions] Social, legal, and institutional barriers limit women’s access to the most productive jobs—shaping the kind of work they do and how it is valued and paid. When women’s skills go unused or underused, economies produce less than they could. In many countries, removing such barriers could increase output by 15 to 20 percent.[reference: gender-distortions]
goal_05.content.#27.text: Let’s have a closer look at some of the challenges faced by women in the workforce from greater concentration in vulnerable employment and unequal pay to growing risks as automation and artificial intelligence reshape the future of work.
goal_05.content.#29.text: To explore these aspects of work, let’s compare the labor force in two very different countries: Switzerland and Tanzania.
goal_05.content.#30.text: Both, Tanzania and Switzerland, have among the highest female labor force participation rates in the world, at nearly 80 percent; male participation is slightly higher in both countries, nearing 90 percent. However, the composition of the labor force in Tanzania, a lower-middle-income country, differs markedly from Switzerland, one of the richest countries in the world.[reference: ilo-employmenttype]
goal_05.content.#31.title: Tale of two countries: Similar labor force participation, different types of employment
goal_05.content.#31.subtitle: Share of population by gender and employment status for Switzerland and Tanzania
goal_05.content.#31.source: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), publisher: ILOSTAT, date accessed: January 07, 2025. Retrieved from Gender Data Portal (SL.EMP.VULN.FE.ZS; SL.EMP.VULN.MA.ZS; SL.EMP.WORK.FE.ZS; SL.EMP.WORK.MA.ZS; SL.UEM.TOTL.FE.ZS; SL.UEM.TOTL.MA.ZS)
goal_05.content.#31.scenes.#0.text: Among the employed, some are wage and salaried workers, while others are in more precarious forms of work, categorized as vulnerable employment.
goal_05.content.#31.scenes.#1.text: In Switzerland, the majority of both men and women are wage and salaried workers— over 8  in 10 men and women. Vulnerable employment is low, with only 1 in 10 employees falling into this category.
goal_05.content.#31.scenes.#2.text: In Tanzania, the pattern is entirely flipped: the labor market is dominated by workers in vulnerable employment, with about 9 in 10 women and 8 in 10 men working in such conditions.
goal_05.content.#31.scenes.#3.text: Only 1 in 10 women hold wage and salaried jobs— half the rate for men with 2 in 10 men in wage and salaried work.
goal_05.content.#32.text: Vulnerable employment includes jobs that typically lack contracts, social protection, and income security. They include contributing family workers—who often help on a family farm and receive no direct pay—and own-account workers, such as street vendors, who work without employees. Two concepts, vulnerable work and informal work, are frequently discussed together. While vulnerable work overlaps with informal work, the distinction matters: informal employment is about the informality of working arrangements, vulnerable employment captures economic insecurity and exposure to risk.[reference: ilo-vulnerable-informal]
goal_05.content.#33.text: High vulnerable employment among women is not unique to Tanzania. In many low-income countries, vulnerable employment rates can reach 90 percent, and women are more likely than men to work in vulnerable jobs—particularly those with more children and therefore greater care responsibilities.[reference: vulnerable-employment]
goal_05.content.#34.title: Vulnerable employment is higher among women than men in low- and lower-middle income countries
goal_05.content.#34.subtitle: Vulnerable employment (% of total employment, modeled ILO estimate), among women and men
goal_05.content.#34.textParagraph: Vulnerable employment is common in low- and middle-income countries, and women are disproportionately affected compared to men.
goal_05.content.#34.source: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), publisher: ILOSTAT, date accessed: January 07, 2025. Retrieved from Gender Data Portal (SL.EMP.VULN.FE.ZS; SL.EMP.VULN.MA.ZS)
goal_05.content.#34.visdescription: Vulnerable employment is common in low income countries. Among countries with the lowest income, vulnerable employment rates are as high as 90 percent. Poorer countries not only have a majority share of vulnerably employed, women tend to have a higher rate than men. <explain why>. In countries with higher incomes, vulnerable employment is less common. The share of men in vulnerable employment also tends to be higher than that of women.
goal_05.content.#34.keyFactLabel: low and middle income countries, vulnerable employment was higher for women than men
goal_05.content.#37.text: By contrast, in high-income countries, vulnerable employment is far less common. Higher education levels and stronger legal protections open the door to more stable jobs.
goal_05.content.#38.text: Although rates are low overall in high-income countries, men are more likely than women to be in vulnerable employment. The drivers of this pattern are not well established. Perhaps, women in high-income countries may be less likely to participate in the labor market when work is vulnerable. And in some cases, measurement may classify certain self‑employed professionals (e.g., accountants or attorneys) as “vulnerable,” which can raise men’s rates without reflecting economic precarity.
goal_05.content.#40.text: Disadvantages for women persist in how their work is valued. This is evident in enduring wage gaps, driven by a range of factors.
goal_05.content.#41.text: Women are often concentrated in lower-paying sectors such as domestic work, clerical services, and contributing work in farms, while men are more likely to hold jobs in higher-paying fields like industry, technology, or formal wage employment. While women have made significant progress in closing education gaps [reference: education-gaps], a combination of skills mismatches, limited access to professional networks and information, and restrictive social norms continues to channel them into lower-productivity occupations.
goal_05.content.#43.text: Even within the same sector, women are less likely to occupy supervisory or leadership positions, limiting their earning potential.[reference: occu-seg]
goal_05.content.#44.title: In most countries, wages are higher for men than women on average
goal_05.content.#44.subtitle: Ratio of women’s wage to men’s wage. A wage ratio higher than 1 signals higher wages for women while a wage ratio lower than 1 signals lower wages for women
goal_05.content.#44.source: Wages and Working Time Statistics (COND), International Labour Organization (ILO), uri: https://ilostat.ilo.org/data/bulk/, publisher: ILOSTAT
goal_05.content.#44.keyFactLabel: for every dollar earned by men in a month
goal_05.content.#44.scenes.#0.text: These dynamics are reflected in earnings: in the average country, women earn just 91 cents for every dollar earned by men.
goal_05.content.#44.scenes.#1.text: Of 131 countries with data, in 93 countries, men are paid more on average than women on an hourly basis.
goal_05.content.#44.scenes.#2.text: The gap widens when measured by monthly earnings. As care and household responsibilities fall disproportionately on women, they are more likely to require flexible work arrangements, work part-time or fewer hours. So, for the average country, women’s monthly earnings were only 82 cents for every dollar earned by men.[footnote: the median country is used as the average country].
goal_05.content.#45.text: There are some limitations with this measure: it shows a basic average across all jobs, so it doesn’t tease out pay differences within the same role. Even so, studies indicate that women can be compensated less than men for the same job. Firms offer greater incentives, sometimes in the form of higher pay, for working longer and fixed hours. This strongly disadvantages women who often bear greater household responsibilities, and require more flexible work arrangements.[reference: goldin-wage]
goal_05.content.#46.text: Narrowing the gender wage gap benefits not only individual women and their economic well-being, but also society as a whole. On average, countries could see a 14 percent increase in wealth by closing the gap in lifetime earnings between men and women.[reference: wage-wealth]
goal_05.content.#48.text: Discussions of employment usually focus on work outside the household. However, a substantial share of productive activity consists of unpaid domestic and care work carried out within households.
goal_05.content.#49.text: Domestic and care work refers to cooking, cleaning and upkeep of a dwelling, shopping, childcare, and care of the sick, elderly or disabled household members, among others.[reference: gdp-timespent] This work is important in daily life and contributes to human capital development and productivity; but it is not equally divided between women and men.
goal_05.content.#50.title: Women spend more time in unpaid work while men spend more time in paid work
goal_05.content.#50.subtitle: Hours spent in a day in paid work and in unpaid work by women and men
goal_05.content.#50.y_axis_title: Time spent on unpaid care work
goal_05.content.#50.scenes.#0.text: Women provide the lion’s share of unpaid work in every single country, while men spend more time in paid work.
goal_05.content.#50.scenes.#1.text: The burden of unpaid work has implications for women’s ability to take up paid work opportunities. Female labor force participation is especially low in countries where women spend many hours on unpaid work.[emphasis:  ]Worldwide more than 708 million women are outside of the labor force due to care responsibilities.[reference: care-tradeoff]
goal_05.content.#50.scenes.#2.text: Paradoxically, even when women have a job, they continue performing most of the unpaid work of the household. In countries with high levels of labor force participation, women face a “second shift”.[reference: second-shift]
goal_05.content.#51.text: Care arrangements differ across societies but in most countries with data available, women’s total workload, encompassing both paid and unpaid work, exceeds that of men. A conservative estimate of the total value of care work provided by women is $11 trillion, equivalent to 9 percent of the global Gross Domestic Product (GDP).[reference: carework-value]
goal_05.content.#52.text: So, on the whole women are typically more time-poor with negative implications for their wellbeing.[reference: total-work] But why do women shoulder a disproportionate amount of unpaid work? Specific reasons vary by family but often include social attitudes and cultural traditions that dictate gendered division of labor, economic inequalities including the gender pay gap, and lack of social services such as childcare. When access to childcare services is expanded, more women can join the labor force and those already in employment are able to work more hours.[reference: childcare-laborforce]
goal_05.content.#54.text: Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping labor markets globally, and the future of jobs is rapidly evolving. AI exposure can have different effects on jobs: displace, enhance or create new jobs. While the full impact remains uncertain, two dynamics are clear. First, AI will automate certain routine tasks, making some jobs redundant. Second, it will increase demand for higher-skilled, technology-enabled roles.
goal_05.content.#55.text: This is disruptive for all workers, especially in high- and upper-middle income countries where AI adoption is more advanced. Based on current technologies, it is estimated that over a third of all jobs in high- and upper-middle income countries could be affected by generative-AI technologies.
goal_05.content.#56.text: In high- and upper-middle income countries the AI-induced disruption will be greater for women than for men. This is because women are concentrated in occupations such as clerical and administrative support which face high automation risk. This raises both the likelihood of job loss and the need for reskilling to transition into new opportunities. In fact, in high-income economies, nearly 10 percent of employed women work in jobs at the highest risk of AI-driven task automation, compared with about 3.5 percent of men. [reference: genai-jobs]
goal_05.content.#57.title: Occupation exposure to AI is highest among women in high- and upper-middle income countries
goal_05.content.#57.subtitle: Occupational exposure to AI by gender and income group
goal_05.content.#57.source: Gmyrek, P., Berg, J., Kamiński, K., Konopczyński, F., Ładna, A., Nafradi, B., Rosłaniec, K., & Troszyński, M. (2025). Generative AI and jobs: A refined global index of occupational exposure (ILO Working Paper No. 140). International Labour Organization. https://doi.org/10.54394/HETP0387
goal_05.content.#58.text: But the diffusion of AI-enabled technologies can also benefit employment of both men and women. New opportunities for high-skilled labor are rising. In fact, in a study of 16 European countries, increased exposure to AI benefited overall employment, and benefited women twice as much as the overall labor force.[reference: ai-women-europe] The largest positive impact on female employment from AI adoption were seen in countries where women have made greater educational progress and already participate more in the labor market.[reference: ai-women-europe]
goal_05.content.#59.text: In low-income countries, AI exposure remains limited for both men and women due to gaps in internet access and reliable electricity.[reference: ai-exposure] However, as digitalization deepens, displacement risks— particularly in the services sector— may rise for both women and men unless proactive measures are taken.
goal_05.content.#60.text: Women in low-income countries also face lower digital literacy and lower access to digital services [reference: findex] which could be a barrier for women to adopt AI in the future.
goal_05.content.#62.text: Artificial Intelligence is already changing the way we work. To meet shifting demand, countries at all income levels must expand efforts to equip young people for emerging roles and reskill the existing workforce in AI-related competencies [reference: ai-unesco]. But skills enhancements alone will not bring more women into the workforce. We also need to fix discriminatory laws—especially those that affect childcare and parental leave,sustain improvements in access to quality education and training for girls and women, and deliberate shifts in social norms that truly support women’s work [reference: halim]. Only with these complementary legal, educational, and societal changes will more women be prepared—and empowered—for the future of work.
goal_05.references.wdi-lfp: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), date accessed: January 07, 2025. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SL.TLF.ACTI.FE.ZS, SL.TLF.ACTI.MA.ZS). https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SL.TLF.ACTI.FE.ZS
goal_05.references.active-work: Per ILO definition, unemployment refers to the share of the labor force that is without work and both available for and seeking employment during a specified reference period. Women may not be available for work during the reference period, as many need to make arrangements for childcare and other familiar duties before starting work, driving down estimates of unemployment among women.
goal_05.references.social-norms: Jayachandran, Seema. (2021). Social Norms as a Barrier to Women’s Employment in Developing Countries. IMF Economic Review. 69. 1-20. 10.1057/s41308-021-00140-w. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/353005285_Social_Norms_as_a_Barrier_to_Women's_Employment_in_Developing_Countries
goal_05.references.progress-paper: Link will be added when working paper version is made available
goal_05.references.turkey-driver: ​​Lugo, Maria Ana; Muller, Miriam; Wai-Poi, Matthew Grant. Middle East and North Africa - Women’s Economic Participation in Iraq, Jordan, and Lebanon (English). Washington, D.C. : World Bank Group. http://documents.worldbank.org/curated/en/933641600751429640

Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SP.DYN.TFRT.IN) https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.DYN.TFRT.IN

goal_05.references.ilo-employmenttype: ILO Modelled Estimates database (ILOEST), International Labour Organization (ILO), uri: https://ilostat.ilo.org/data/bulk/, publisher: ILOSTAT

Engendering informality statistics: gaps and opportunities: Working paper to support revision of the standards for statistics on informality, ILO Working Paper (Geneva, ILO). https://www.ilo.org/sites/default/files/wcmsp5/groups/public/%40dgreports/%40stat/documents/publication/wcms_861882.pdf

Lo Bue, Tu Thi Ngoc Le, Manuel Santos Silva, Kunal Sen, Gender and vulnerable employment in the developing world: Evidence from global microdata, World Development, Volume 159, 2022, 106010, ISSN 0305-750X, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2022.106010.

goal_05.references.second-shift: Hochschild, A. R., & Machung, A. (1989). The second shift: Working parents and the revolution at home. Viking. https://catalogue.nla.gov.au/catalog/2059607
goal_05.references.total-work: Paid plus unpaid work high for women… ILO?
